recommendations.component_name: cbm-session-reminder SessionStart hook vs CLAUDE.md codebase-memory rule (dueling instructions)
recommendations.current_purpose: Hook injects 'CRITICAL - Code Discovery Protocol: ALWAYS use codebase-memory-mcp tools FIRST for ANY code exploration ... If a project is not indexed yet, run index_repository FIRST' at every SessionStart. CLAUDE.md:88 exists specifically to counteract it: scoped to LARGE/unfamiliar repos only, ALL results ADVISORY (4/5 no-silent-pass probe failures), skip for small/single-file.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: The CLAUDE.md rule is evidence-backed (probe failures: dead-code false-positive, stale-by-default, parse-degradation, cp950 mojibake) and correctly scoped. The hook does drive tool adoption.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Every session receives two directly contradictory instructions on the same topic: hook says 'ALWAYS ... FIRST for ANY code exploration' with no caveat; CLAUDE.md:88 says skip for small/single-file, treat all results as advisory, never trust index_status:ready. CLAUDE.md:88 itself acknowledges the contradiction ('A SessionStart hook injects a blunter always-use-it-first with no caveat — THIS rule is the scoped, advisory-correct form'). Keeping a rule whose sole job is to override another injected rule is pure patch-on-patch; it burns per-session tokens and creates ambiguity about which instruction wins.
recommendations.expected_impact: Edit the hook text to the scoped/advisory form (it is a 16-line local bash script, trivially editable), then delete the counter-rule caveat sentence from CLAUDE.md:88. Removes a standing P0-class instruction conflict and ~3 lines of prose whose only function is contradiction management.
recommendations.suggested_test: Start a fresh session, capture the SessionStart injection, confirm hook text now matches CLAUDE.md scoping; then run a small-single-file task probe and confirm the agent greps directly instead of indexing.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If only the CLAUDE.md rule is deleted (without fixing the hook), the unscoped 'ALWAYS use it first' wins and the advisory-verification discipline (verify who-calls-X with grep/tests) is lost — that discipline is evidence-backed. Fix must land in the hook first.

recommendations.component_name: DELEGATION.md REJECT path instructs deny-listed `git checkout --`
recommendations.component_type: workflow instruction vs machine enforcement conflict
recommendations.current_purpose: Multi-agent commit-discipline table: on REJECT of a delegate's output, 'git checkout -- <changed-files> to discard → re-delegate'.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: The REJECT-and-discard step itself is sound (a bad agent's output must be dropped before re-delegation).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Verified via settings.json: `Bash(git checkout --:*)` is in the permissions deny list (command output in this audit), and AGENTS.md:93 lists it under '🚫 Never (machine-enforced)'. The mandated recovery workflow instructs a command the harness blocks — it will fail exactly at the recovery moment of a multi-agent round. `git restore` is NOT in the deny list and achieves the same discard.
recommendations.expected_impact: One-line fix: change DELEGATION.md:198 to `git restore <changed-files>` (or `git stash push <files>` for archive-not-delete consistency). Unblocks the REJECT path of every future multi-agent round.
recommendations.suggested_test: In a scratch repo, run the documented REJECT sequence verbatim and confirm `git checkout --` is blocked while `git restore` succeeds; update the doc and re-run.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: None from the edit itself. Note `git restore` discards working-tree changes just as destructively — if the deny on `git checkout --` was meant to block that CLASS of operation, the deny list has a hole and the policy owner should decide intent.

recommendations.component_name: AGENTS.md §Permissions overstates machine enforcement (rm, git merge not in deny list)
recommendations.current_purpose: Declares a '🚫 Never (machine-enforced for Claude via settings.json deny list)' tier including `rm`/`rm -rf` (line 90), `git merge` directly (line 96); line 103 attributes 'Destructive git ops' enforcement to the settings.json PreToolUse deny list.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Most of the tier IS machine-enforced — verified in settings.json deny: git push --force/-f, git reset --hard, git checkout --, git clean -f, git branch -D, .env/secrets reads+writes. The portable-contract framing for non-Claude agents (lines 101-107) is genuinely useful.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Verified settings.json deny list contains NO entry for `rm`, NO entry for `git merge`, and no Write deny for ./secrets/** (only Read). So two 'machine-enforced' claims are false: an agent (or operator) trusting the documented backstop for rm/git merge has none. Stale-enforcement claims are exactly the rot AGENTS.md:118 already had to correct once (the phantom worktree-cleanup task, 'an earlier doc claimed one, corrected 2026-05-19').
recommendations.expected_impact: Re-tier rm/git merge as prose-discipline (or actually add them to deny — a policy decision for the user). Restores the file's core value proposition: the enforcement column being truthful.
recommendations.suggested_test: Add a parity check (extend scripts/audit_settings.py or a new 20-line script) that diffs AGENTS.md's 'machine-enforced' bullet list against settings.json permissions.deny and fails on mismatch; run it in /ai-review.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If instead the claims are silently deleted, non-Claude agents lose the 'never rm' signal entirely — the fix is correcting the enforcement attribution, not removing the rules.

recommendations.component_name: Two review-gate sections: 'Pre-Commit Agent Review (Mandatory)' vs 'Mandatory code-reviewer subagent' (+ 'Narrow exceptions')
recommendations.current_purpose: Section A (46 lines): before every commit, check a 5-trigger table; if fired, invoke the `code-review` SKILL (Skill tool) and gate on its verdict. Section B (45 lines incl. exceptions): before committing ANY shipping output, spawn the `code-reviewer` SUBAGENT (Agent tool), with its own task-type table and two narrow exceptions.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Incident provenance is real and cited: v0.88.15 (4 P1 bugs behind 2376 green tests, CLAUDE.md:122,155), 66be639 over-stage (CLAUDE.md:121,131), and DELEGATION.md:256-264 catalogs 7 failure classes the reviewer caught. A review gate demonstrably earns its keep.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: This is substantially one rule written twice with a mechanism fork. Quantified: the two sections are 92 of 215 lines = 43% of CLAUDE.md. Trigger-surface overlap ~70%: multi-file commits (A trigger#1 ↔ B row1), ≥50-LOC single file (A#1 ↔ B row2), post-delegate diff (A#4 ↔ B hard-exclusion line 197), governance files (A#5 ↔ B hard-exclusions lines 194-196), and B explicitly imports A's escape-hatch list (lines 189-192: 'the Escape-hatch list applies to this table too'). Distinct residue: A adds release/critical-path triggers + Co-Reviewed-By recording; B adds the ≥2-file row and the 2 narrow exceptions. Read literally, a ≥3-file commit requires BOTH a code-review Skill run AND a code-reviewer subagent spawn (plus, where installed, the pre-commit hook + ack script per AGENTS.md:144-149) — 3-4 review layers on one commit with no evidence the skill and subagent catch disjoint bug classes (each section's catch-catalog is attributed to its own mechanism: CLAUDE.md:155 to the skill, DELEGATION.md:256 to the subagent).
recommendations.expected_impact: Merge into ONE gate section: one trigger table (union of A+B triggers, one file-count threshold), one escape-hatch list, one mechanism (or an explicit rule for when skill vs subagent). Estimated ~40-50 line reduction (~20% of the file) and removal of the double-review ambiguity/latency on every multi-file commit.
recommendations.suggested_test: Take the last 10 gated commits from git history/logs, record which section(s) fired and whether two reviews ran; then re-run 3 of them against the merged single-gate draft and confirm identical block/pass decisions with one review invocation.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Merging must not drop unique triggers (release commits, critical path, governance hard-exclusions) — each has a named incident. Risk is in the merge, not the concept; do it under trigger #5 governance review.

recommendations.component_name: File-count threshold drift: ≥3 files vs ≥2 files vs ≥50-lines-only
recommendations.current_purpose: Defines when a commit is 'non-trivial' enough to require review.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: A numeric tripwire is the load-bearing anti-rationalization device (CLAUDE.md:96: catching yourself reclassifying IS the signal).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Four inconsistent statements of the same threshold: CLAUDE.md:121 trigger#1 '≥50 LOC OR ≥3 files'; CLAUDE.md:163 subagent table 'Multi-file commit (≥ 2 files) → Mandatory'; AGENTS.md:145 hook 'diff ≥ 50 lines OR ≥3 files'; AGENTS.md:82 ask-first 'git commit when staged diff ≥ 50 lines' (no file-count clause at all). A 2-file/20-LOC commit is simultaneously below trigger#1, below the hook, below ask-first — yet Mandatory per line 163. This is live drift of the kind the 66be639 postmortem warned about (stale renumbered/mismatched references, DELEGATION.md:264).
recommendations.expected_impact: Pick ONE threshold, state it once, and have all other locations reference it by name ('trigger #1'). Eliminates the contradiction and future drift surface.
recommendations.suggested_test: grep -n for '≥ ?[23] files|≥ ?50' across CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md/DELEGATION.md/git-hooks after the edit; assert exactly one canonical definition plus named references.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If harmonized upward to ≥3 files, 2-file commits lose their mandatory subagent — decide deliberately which number is the real policy before harmonizing.

recommendations.component_name: Gemini deprecation residue (45 lines, 16 tombstones, dead CLI example)
recommendations.current_purpose: Tombstones the 2026-06-18 Gemini CLI deprecation so work is not routed to a fail-closed wrapper.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: One tombstone per file IS necessary: the gemini-delegate skill is still installed (verified: ~/.claude/skills/gemini-delegate exists) and still appears in the session skill listing, so a routing guard has a real trigger. The wrapper failing closed is a backstop, not a substitute for the redirect rule (CJK→Claude-direct/Codex).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Measured: 45 Gemini-mentioning lines across the three files (CLAUDE.md 10, AGENTS.md 6, DELEGATION.md 29); the exact string 'DEPRECATED 2026-06-18' appears 16 times (5 in CLAUDE.md, 11 in DELEGATION.md). CLAUDE.md:3 still self-describes as holding 'codex/gemini routing tables'; the section title at line 90 still says 'Codex/Gemini = Executors'. DELEGATION.md:31-43 preserves a 13-line dead invocation example (full `cat | gemini --yolo` command) for a CLI that no longer exists — the transferable F1 lesson ('delegate CLIs may honor gitignore; pipe briefs via stdin') fits in one line. The skill listing itself already carries the DEPRECATED tag redundantly.
recommendations.expected_impact: Reduce to one tombstone per file + strip Gemini from table cells and the header self-description; collapse DELEGATION.md:31-43 to a 2-line generalized F1 lesson. ~30 lines removed; every remaining Gemini mention becomes load-bearing.
recommendations.suggested_test: After slimming: grep -c gemini per file (target ≤3 lines each); then run a routing probe ('translate this 300-line zh-TW doc') and confirm the agent routes Claude-direct/Codex without attempting gemini-delegate.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Removing ALL tombstones would risk re-routing to the dead lane (fails closed = wasted round + confusing error, not silent corruption). Keep exactly one per file; also consider uninstalling/archiving skills/gemini-delegate to kill the trigger at the source.

recommendations.component_name: 'Self-enforced policy — no hook blocks it' vs documented pre-commit review-gate hook
recommendations.current_purpose: Line 115 motivates the prose gate by claiming no mechanical enforcement exists; lines 25 and AGENTS.md:126/144-149 document a per-repo pre-commit hook that blocks exactly trigger #1 (verified: ~/.claude/git-hooks/pre-commit-review-gate exists).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Both statements are individually true-ish: the hook is per-repo opt-in, so unhooked repos rely on prose. The prose gate covers the gap.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: As written they flatly contradict: 'no hook blocks it' (115) vs 'pre-commit review-gate (blocks when trigger #1 fires...)' (25). A reader (or model) can cite line 115 to argue the gate is aspirational in a repo where the hook IS installed, or over-trust enforcement where it isn't. Classic old-patch layering: line 115 predates the hook's installation story.
recommendations.expected_impact: One-clause fix: 'no GLOBAL hook blocks it; the per-repo review-gate hook enforces trigger #1 where installed (see AGENTS.md §Commit gates)'. Removes the contradiction.
recommendations.suggested_test: Read-through check: grep 'no hook' CLAUDE.md returns the corrected clause; verify against `git config core.hooksPath`/hook presence in one gated and one ungated repo.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: None — pure wording correction.

recommendations.component_name: Worktree 'merge if good' vs AGENTS.md 'Never git merge directly'
recommendations.current_purpose: CLAUDE.md:10: 'After every task: merge if good, delete if not' (worktree discipline). AGENTS.md:96: '🚫 Never ... git merge directly (use PR workflow — the deny exists to force this)'.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Both intents are individually sensible: don't leave worktrees lingering; keep merges auditable via PRs.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Direct contradiction for the routine end-of-task path: the worktree rule instructs a local merge; the portable contract forbids it and claims a deny 'forces' PR workflow — and (verified) NO git-merge deny exists in settings.json, so the claimed forcing function is also fictional (see the enforcement-overstatement finding). An agent finishing a worktree task cannot satisfy both lines.
recommendations.expected_impact: Decide the real policy (likely: small/direct-to-main work never branches; branch work merges via PR or fast-forward with review) and state it in ONE place. Removes a both-ways-you-lose instruction pair.
recommendations.suggested_test: Scenario probe: give a fresh session a finished worktree branch and ask it to close out the task; observe whether it merges, PRs, or stalls asking — before and after the harmonized wording.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If 'merge if good' is simply deleted, worktrees will linger (the exact failure line 10 exists to prevent); if 'never merge' is deleted, audit-trail intent is lost. Needs a deliberate policy pick, not deletion.

recommendations.component_name: Verbatim duplication between CLAUDE.md and DELEGATION.md (tripwire, subagent rationale, invocation, acceptance-gate presets)
recommendations.current_purpose: Header design (CLAUDE.md:3, DELEGATION.md:4-8) says CLAUDE.md keeps per-turn 'decision cores' and DELEGATION.md holds reference detail loaded on demand.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: The split architecture itself is sound and matches Anthropic's anti-bloat guidance quoted in the header; per-turn context does need the trigger conditions.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: The 'core vs detail' boundary has eroded into copies: the delegation tripwire paragraph is near-verbatim in both (CLAUDE.md:96 ↔ DELEGATION.md:27); the sentence "Recurring drift's root cause is: operator commits, then 'remembers' to review later, then never does" appears verbatim in both (CLAUDE.md:159 ↔ DELEGATION.md:254); the Agent invocation pattern is duplicated (CLAUDE.md:171 ↔ DELEGATION.md:269-274); all three acceptance-gate presets WITH their full CLI args AND the 3-item skip list are duplicated (CLAUDE.md:209-213 ↔ DELEGATION.md:224-235). Duplication is the drift engine — the threshold-drift finding above shows it already producing divergence.
recommendations.expected_impact: Keep trigger CONDITIONS in CLAUDE.md, move all invocation syntax/args/rationale to DELEGATION.md only, referenced by pointer (the file already uses this pattern elsewhere, e.g. line 108). ~15-20 lines out of per-turn context and one fewer mirror to keep in sync.
recommendations.suggested_test: Post-edit: a duplicate-paragraph detector (e.g. 12-word shingle overlap) between the two files reports zero shared blocks >1 line; routing probe confirms the preset still fires on a mock multi-locale diff.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Preset triggers must remain visible per-turn (the DeepSeek-R2 fabrication shipped because the check wasn't in the loop, CLAUDE.md:209) — dedupe the args/detail, not the trigger conditions.

recommendations.component_name: Per-repo/human context in global per-turn file (MoodRing strategy, Eric-phone reporting, ETF rule)
recommendations.current_purpose: MoodRing branch/validation strategy (40-43); 'Include dashboard link in morning briefing (Eric checks on phone)' + 'Always run pipeline AFTER code fixes' (61-62); 'ETF-only trading' (85, filed under the Delegation heading).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Each rule is plausibly real operator preference; the ETF rule is a genuine safety boundary for trading tasks; AGENTS.md:173 explicitly assigns 'human context (Eric checks dashboard on phone)' to CLAUDE.md, so this placement is by design.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: These load into EVERY session in every repo — including this harness repo, academic-writing sessions, etc. — where MoodRing pipeline/dashboard rules are noise. Per-repo override files exist and are documented as the mechanism (AGENTS.md:32,176 'Per-repo CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md — repo-specific overrides'). The ETF rule under a 'Delegation' heading is also mis-filed, weakening findability exactly when a trading task appears. This is the low-grade bloat the header itself warns causes rule-ignoring.
recommendations.expected_impact: Move MoodRing strategy + pipeline/dashboard reporting lines to moodring/CLAUDE.md; move ETF-only to a one-line 'Trading boundary' bullet or the personal-context block. ~8 lines out of global per-turn context, better rule locality.
recommendations.suggested_test: UNVERIFIED whether moodring repo has its own CLAUDE.md (outside audit scope) — test = confirm per-repo file exists and contains the moved rules, then run a MoodRing session probe checking the validation guardrail still fires.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If the MoodRing repo lacks a CLAUDE.md, the guardrail (validate docs/ JSON before push — bad push = broken live dashboard) would vanish; verify/create the repo file in the same change. ETF rule must not be dropped, only relocated.

recommendations.component_name: Per-task mechanical git rituals in prose ('checkout main && pull' before EVERY task; 'worktree prune' after EVERY task)
recommendations.current_purpose: Ensure every task starts from latest main and no stale worktrees accumulate.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Worktree accumulation is a real failure mode (the rule's existence implies past pain; AGENTS.md:118 confirms it is deliberately manual after a phantom-automation correction 2026-05-19).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: (a) Violates the file's OWN design principle (CLAUDE.md:3 'if a rule is mechanical, codify it (hook / script / scheduled task), don't write it here'; AGENTS.md:133-134 'Codify what's enforceable'). (b) 'git checkout main && git pull' before ANY task is wrong for parallel sessions/worktrees (this environment spawns worktree sessions) and for read-only tasks — it adds two network-touching commands of latency per task and can yank a session off an experiment branch, directly under step 3 which then asks whether to branch. (c) The prune commands are pure fixed strings — exactly what a SessionEnd hook or the existing nightly auto_sync (AGENTS.md:116) could run.
recommendations.expected_impact: Move prune into auto_sync.sh or a SessionEnd hook; soften checkout-main to 'when starting NEW work on main, pull first'. Removes 2 commands × every task of latency and a parallel-session hazard; 4-5 lines of prose become 1.
recommendations.suggested_test: Add prune to auto_sync Step 1.5 and verify via its log for a week; scenario probe a resumed-worktree session and confirm it no longer force-checkouts main.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Stale-worktree rot returns if automation isn't actually landed before prose removal — sequence: codify first, then slim (per the header's own doctrine).

recommendations.component_name: Multi-agent .coord/ orchestration mandate (task-splitter → reconciler → acceptance-gate pipeline)
recommendations.current_purpose: For ≥2 parallel delegates: mandatory agent-task-splitter, per-agent commits, reconciler, acceptance gate, 7-skill routing table (~110 lines across the files).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: The acceptance-gate presets are incident-born: 2026-05-13 DeepSeek-R2 fabrication shipped precisely because fact-check-frontier-models didn't exist (CLAUDE.md:209, DELEGATION.md:230). Commit-boundary discipline gives surgical revert (DELEGATION.md:202-206).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: AGENTS.md:30 (its own audit): '.coord/ ... Currently unused — 0 of 7 audited repos have a .coord/ dir as of 2026-05-14'. Additionally, the ≥2-parallel-delegate precondition became rarer after the Gemini lane died 2026-06-18 (the pipeline's canonical example was codex+gemini, DELEGATION.md:185 still says 'codex + gemini'). ~110 lines of mandate for a path with zero recorded activations as of the last audit is heavy scaffolding; the unused-note itself is now ~7 weeks stale (today 2026-07-06).
recommendations.expected_impact: Re-audit .coord/ presence now; if still 0 activations, compress CLAUDE.md:203-215 to ~4 lines (decision rule + pointer + the three preset trigger conditions, which protect single-delegate rounds too) and let DELEGATION.md Part 2 carry the rest. ~8 per-turn lines saved without deleting the machinery.
recommendations.suggested_test: Run `ls -d <repo>/.coord 2>/dev/null` across the 8 active repos + grep session logs for agent-task-splitter invocations since 2026-05-14; decide keep-vs-compress on the measured activation count.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: The presets protect against a demonstrated fabrication class even in 1-delegate rounds — they must survive any compression. Full deletion would remove the only orchestration playbook if multi-delegate work resumes.

recommendations.component_name: Model/Skill Routing — Fable distilled benchmark section
recommendations.current_purpose: Routes when to add the Fable distilled skill/harness on top of Claude/Codex baselines (high-risk completion claims, root-cause, governance, etc.).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Opens with the right anti-ceremony default ('do not wrap every task in Fable'); consistent with the harness-repo philosophy; routing by task risk is cheap to keep.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Project memory (MEMORY.md → harness-ab-ceilings-on-opus.md) records that skill-effect A/B hit ceilings — 88-100% plain-A across every scenario type, 'don't re-run expecting a measurable effect'. I.e., the measured evidence base could NOT demonstrate the differential benefit this routing table asserts for 'Opus 4.8 + Fable' rows; the claimed benefit is currently unfalsified belief, not benchmark result. UNVERIFIED: whether the Arm-C self-load probe (noted in memory as the ceiling-immune design) has since produced positive evidence.
recommendations.expected_impact: Keep the section but annotate rows as hypothesis-grade pending Arm-C results; if Arm-C also shows no effect, collapse to 2 lines (load GLOBAL_BOOTSTRAP.md for large/multi-agent/phase-gated work — which line 64-70 already says, making 72-80 partially redundant with the Fable-style Harness section directly above it).
recommendations.suggested_test: Run the Arm-C self-load probe already identified in memory as ceiling-immune; gate this section's survival on that result.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If the harness DOES change outcomes on high-risk tasks (ceiling-masked, not absent), removing routing loses the benefit exactly where false-done is expensive.

recommendations.component_name: Incident-born gate core (release trigger, scope-assertion, stdout-redirect rule, orphan-pytest rule, staged-filecount rule)
recommendations.current_purpose: The rules with named, dated incident provenance: release-commit review (v0.88.15: 4 P1 bugs behind 2376 green tests), commit-msg scope assertion (66be639: 46 files under a 2-file subject), never-redirect codex stdout (7 GB log 2026-04-17), no background pytest on Windows (~42 GB orphan accumulation 2026-05-20), hook fork-cost discipline (d5f0bde), fact-check presets (DeepSeek-R2 2026-05-13).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Each has a concrete failure it demonstrably caught or would have caught, with cost quantified in the doc itself (CLAUDE.md:155 lists 5 consecutive releases whose bugs only retroactive review found). The trigger conditions (releases, commits, codex runs, pytest on Windows) all still occur routinely. Several are ALSO machine-enforced (git-hooks verified present: pre-commit-review-gate, commit-msg-scope-assertion; hooks verified present: check_no_verify.py, check_stdout_redirect.py) — prose + hook layering is intentional for repos without hooks installed.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: None found for the boundaries themselves. Only their PACKAGING is bloated (see the dual-gate and duplication findings) — e.g. the 11-row anti-pattern table (129-137) carries full incident narratives per-turn where a vault-rule pointer would do.
recommendations.expected_impact: These are the load-bearing 30% of the corpus; any slim pass must be scored against preserving every one of these triggers (this is the regression suite for the cleanup itself).
recommendations.suggested_test: Before/after any CLAUDE.md slim: replay the 6 incident scenarios as prompts against the edited corpus and verify the agent still refuses/blocks/reviews in each (the harness's claude_md_eval.py at ~/.claude/scripts/ looks purpose-built for this).
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Direct recurrence of the named incidents — each cost hours-to-days (7 GB / 42 GB cleanups, 4 shipped P1s, near-shipped fabrication).

recommendations.component_name: Overall CLAUDE.md size/rigidity posture
recommendations.current_purpose: Per-turn global instruction file; header (line 3) explicitly commits to the slim doctrine and quotes Anthropic's bloat warning.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Measured: 215 lines is genuinely post-diet (module files hold 959-215=744 lines of detail; MAINTENANCE/ACADEMIC_WRITING are correctly pointer-ized to 9 and 8 lines respectively at CLAUDE.md:28-36,45-52). The architecture (core + on-demand modules + hooks) matches best practice.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Density metrics show residual old-patch character: 26 lines contain mandatory/must/never/always; 4 tables / 31 table rows; 43% of the file (lines 110-201) is review-gating with a mechanism fork; ~10 lines are contradiction-management or stale self-description (gemini header claim line 3, 'no hook blocks it' line 115, cbm counter-rule line 88). The bloat is concentrated, not diffuse — 5 targeted edits (findings above) recover ~60-80 lines without touching any incident-born boundary.
recommendations.expected_impact: Target ~140-155 lines: merge the two gates, single tombstone per file, relocate per-repo content, pointer-ize duplicated preset args. Directly serves the header's own stated goal (less bloat → better rule adherence).
recommendations.suggested_test: Run ~/.claude/scripts/claude_md_eval.py (referenced in AGENTS.md:129 automation map) before/after; plus the 6-incident replay suite from the Keep finding.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Over-aggressive slimming that drops trigger conditions (not just detail) would recreate the pre-gate failure modes; the edit itself is governance (trigger #5) and needs full review.

recommendations.component_name: F14 blocking PreToolUse hook (check_codex_skill_routing.py)
recommendations.current_purpose: Blocks raw `codex exec` / `gemini -p` shipping tasks (exit 2), forcing routing through Skill(codex-delegate); also blocks deprecated flags --full-auto and -m gpt-5.4 unconditionally.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Telemetry: 27 block_routing + 19 block_deprecated_flag events in ~/.claude/logs/hook_events.jsonl (latest 2026-06-20). History: 99% bypass rate (181 raw vs 2 skill calls) when it was warn-only (docstring lines 18-21). Escape hatches exist for legitimate cases (lines 27-32).
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: One telemetry entry (2026-06-18) shows a block on `Select-String -Path ...run_codex.ps1 ...` — a read-only search over a file whose path contains 'codex', suggesting matcher over-reach (only cmd_prefix visible; exact cause UNVERIFIED). Also SKILL.md:60 still describes this hook as 'non-blocking (warns only)', so the doc and reality have drifted.
recommendations.expected_impact: Keeping preserves the only mechanical enforcement of the routing rule; tightening the matcher (exclude commands where 'codex'/'gemini' appear only inside a path argument) removes false-positive friction.
recommendations.suggested_test: Replay the 2026-06-18 Select-String command through the hook (echo the PreToolUse JSON into it) and assert exit 0; replay `codex exec --sandbox workspace-write "do X"` and assert exit 2.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Documented regression to 99% bypass: raw codex exec loses stdin closure (25-min hang incident), .result.json contract, and brief traceability (F11/F12 drift).

recommendations.component_name: Tripwire threshold (≥3 files CLAUDE.md vs ≥5 files SKILL.md snippet)
recommendations.current_purpose: CLAUDE.md:96 makes delegation MANDATORY at ≥3 files of the same mechanical transform; the skill's embeddable CLAUDE.md snippet says ≥5 files (skills/codex-delegate/skills/codex-delegate/SKILL.md:50).
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: A hard numeric tripwire counters the documented failure mode of reclassifying mechanical bulk as 'judgment' to avoid delegating (CLAUDE.md:96, DELEGATION.md:27); the ≥3-file condition also aligns with the pre-commit review-gate's ≥3-files trigger.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: The two numbers conflict (3 vs 5), and neither is backed by measurement: the skill's own savings table (SKILL.md:24-33) has no data below 8 files; smallest measured win is 17-22× at 8 files, ~1× at single-file. The 3-5 file band is unmeasured.
recommendations.expected_impact: Pick one number (state it once in CLAUDE.md, have SKILL.md reference it) and note it as provisional pending a 3-5-file A/B; eliminates a live contradiction an agent could exploit either direction.
recommendations.suggested_test: grep both files for the threshold and assert a single canonical number; then time/token-measure one real 3-file and one 5-file mechanical sweep via codex-delegate vs Claude-direct.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If the tripwire is dropped entirely, the anti-pattern it was built for (keeping mechanical bulk in-Claude) returns; if only reconciled, risk is nil.

recommendations.component_name: Model/Skill Routing row: 'Codex + Fable for governance changes / root-cause work'
recommendations.current_purpose: CLAUDE.md:79 tells the operator to use Codex+Fable-distilled for 'provider/tooling debugging, complex multi-step recovery, governance changes, or root-cause work'.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: The underlying intent (Fable harness improves Codex discipline on hard orchestration) is a benchmarked observation from the distillation project; the row documents when the Fable overlay is worth its runtime cost.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: It directly contradicts the routing table 24 lines later (CLAUDE.md:103: governance → Claude direct, LOC irrelevant) and DELEGATION.md:123-126 (bug diagnosis, CLAUDE.md/governance updates = bad for Codex). An agent reading only the Model/Skill Routing section could route a governance change to Codex.
recommendations.expected_impact: Reword to scope the row to Codex-native work environments (Hermes/Codex-primary sessions), e.g. 'when work is already in the Codex lane and needs discipline', and add 'governance in Claude-managed repos stays Claude-direct per Complex Task Protocol'. Removes the contradiction without losing the benchmark insight.
recommendations.suggested_test: Ask a fresh agent session 'a settings.json hook needs a fix — which executor?' with only CLAUDE.md loaded; correct answer must be Claude-direct with no hesitation sourced from line 79.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: Removing the row entirely loses the documented answer to 'when is Fable-on-Codex worth it', which the distilled benchmark section exists to encode.

recommendations.component_name: Codex-must-not-commit rule (prose-only, no mechanical guard)
recommendations.current_purpose: DELEGATION.md:196 ('no commit by agent itself') and CLAUDE.md:106 anti-pattern reserve commit/push authority to Claude after diff review.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Commit-per-agent-boundary discipline (DELEGATION.md:189-206) depends on it: surgical revert, git blame attribution, and acceptance-gate verifying committed state all break if the agent self-commits.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: Not obsolete — under-enforced. `codex exec --sandbox workspace-write` auto-approves commands (SKILL.md:114) and can write .git; the brief template Constraints (task-template.md:22-25) lack a no-commit line; run_codex.sh only snapshots `git status --porcelain` (lines 199-203), so a Codex-side commit would silently empty files_changed instead of failing.
recommendations.expected_impact: Add (a) 'Do not run git commit/push/tag' to the template Constraints and (b) a HEAD-sha before/after assertion in run_codex.sh that flips result.json status to error if HEAD moved. Closes the only path by which Codex could become de-facto final author.
recommendations.suggested_test: Craft a brief instructing Codex to `git commit -am test` in a scratch repo; run the wrapper; assert result.json reports the HEAD move as error (post-fix) vs today's silent success (pre-fix).
recommendations.risk_if_changed: N/A for removal; if left as-is, a single Codex self-commit would corrupt the audit chain (files_changed empty, review gate sees clean tree) without any alarm.

recommendations.component_name: Trigger #4 (post-delegate review) enforcement gap
recommendations.current_purpose: CLAUDE.md:124 makes code-review mandatory whenever a Codex/Gemini delegate returns; it is the review half of the author-agnostic gate.
recommendations.evidence_it_still_helps: Post-delegate diffs are exactly where F11/F12-class drift ships; the reviewer catalog (DELEGATION.md:256-264) lists real catches. Indirect mechanical backup exists: tripwire work is ≥3 files, which also fires the per-repo pre-commit review-gate.
recommendations.evidence_it_may_be_obsolete: The trigger table is explicitly 'Self-enforced policy — no hook blocks it' (CLAUDE.md §Pre-Commit Agent Review), and the pre-commit gate is per-repo, not global — a Codex diff in a repo without the gate ships on prose discipline alone.
recommendations.expected_impact: Cheap mechanical closure: have run_codex.sh drop a `.git/CODEX_DELEGATE_PENDING` marker on success; the existing pre-commit review-gate (where installed) or a small commit-msg hook refuses to commit while the marker exists and no ack file does. Converts trigger #4 from prose to gate in delegate-heavy repos.
recommendations.suggested_test: In a scratch repo with the gate installed, run a wrapper task then attempt `git commit` without the ack; assert the commit is blocked until claude-ack-review.sh runs.
recommendations.risk_if_changed: If trigger #4 were dropped, unreviewed delegate output could ship in small-diff cases (<50 LOC, <3 files) that dodge trigger #1 entirely.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:36-38 runs `node statusline_pro.mjs think` on EVERY user prompt ('*' matcher). Script (hooks/statusline_pro.mjs:29-53) execSyncs 5 git subprocesses (rev-parse x3, status --porcelain, rev-list) + reads .mcp.json, then draws an ANSI status overlay to stderr.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Near zero today. It renders a status line, but settings.json:168-172 already wires a dedicated statusLine (coralline/statusline.sh, added Jun 16; statusline_pro.mjs is dated Feb 11 — predates it). UserPromptSubmit stderr from an exit-0 hook does not reliably render in the TUI (check_redundant_reads.py:28-29 documents that exit-0 stderr is not surfaced).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Measured 244 ms per fire (node cold-start + 5 git forks) on this machine, i.e. ~55% of the ~450 ms total UserPromptSubmit hook latency, plus ~6 process forks per prompt on Windows where fork is expensive.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: No — it duplicates the coralline statusLine; nothing to merge into.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: Already deterministic; the replacement (coralline statusline.sh) already exists and is wired.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: N/A — removal is strictly better than caching.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: Yes. This is the single clearest violation of the user's own d5f0bde rule ('lightweight Python observer tolerated, Node is not' on high-frequency matchers, CLAUDE.md anti-pattern table) — Node + execSync git on a '*' matcher.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: ~244 ms and ~6 forks saved per user prompt; hundreds of prompts/day ≈ minutes/day of dead latency removed with zero functionality loss.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Very low. Only risk is the user actually sees and likes the stderr overlay in some terminal; a one-week trial removal answers that.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Delete the entry from settings.json UserPromptSubmit, run one session, confirm the coralline statusLine still renders and nothing else changes; time a UserPromptSubmit round-trip before/after (expect ~-240 ms).

inefficient_invocations.invocation_name: PreToolUse(Bash) quartet: check_stdout_redirect + check_codex_skill_routing + check_no_verify + check_redundant_bash
inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:74-93 spawns FOUR separate python processes on every single Bash tool call. Each parses the same stdin JSON, does the same tool_name check, and three of the four import the same _event_log helper.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Individually real: codex routing block (46 logged events), redundant-bash block (25 blocks; motivating incident was 108x identical pytest, check_redundant_bash.py:7-9), no-verify telemetry (17 events). See separate findings for check_stdout_redirect (near-zero value).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Measured ~70 ms per python hook ⇒ ~280 ms of pure interpreter startup per Bash call. The user's own evidence base (check_redundant_bash.py:8) records a session with 2,262 Bash calls — at 3 avoidable extra interpreters that is ~7.9 minutes of dead startup time in ONE session.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes — the highest-value merge in the harness. All four are stdin-JSON → regex/state-check → exit 0/2 with identical envelopes. A single dispatcher (bash_gate.py) running the four checks in sequence preserves exact exit-code semantics (first non-zero wins, exit 2 blocks).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: They ARE deterministic code; the inefficiency is process-count, not logic.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — these exist precisely because LLM self-discipline failed (99% raw-codex bypass rate, check_codex_skill_routing.py:17-21).
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: No per-fire caching needed beyond the existing temp-file session state.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: ~200-210 ms saved per Bash call (4 interpreters → 1); on heavy sessions (2,000+ Bash calls) this is minutes per session and thousands fewer Windows forks.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Moderate: a bug in the dispatcher degrades all four gates at once. Mitigate by keeping each check as an importable module function and reusing the existing hooks/tests suite.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Pipe recorded payloads (a raw `codex exec`, a `git commit -n`, an .ai/ redirect, a 10th identical command) into the merged dispatcher and assert identical exit codes + stderr text vs the four originals; then run the existing hooks/tests directory.

inefficient_invocations.invocation_name: UserPromptSubmit python trio: check_numbered_list + check_correction_phrases + inject_method_brain
inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:40-52 spawns three python processes on every user prompt, each re-parsing the same prompt payload and each emitting at most one additionalContext JSON.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: check_numbered_list: 546 injects logged (F15, real behavior fix). inject_method_brain: 2,000 injects — the causal delivery mechanism for the method-brain vault. check_correction_phrases: near-dead (see separate finding).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~200 ms (3 × ~67 ms measured) interpreter startup per prompt, every prompt, every session.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes — trivially. All three follow the identical template (same file header lineage; check_correction_phrases.py:9-10 even names the others as siblings). One prompt_gate.py can run all three matchers and print up to N hookSpecificOutput blocks (or concatenate additionalContext strings).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — the whole point is injecting context BEFORE the model reasons.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: inject_method_brain's rule index should be disk-cached (see its own finding).
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: ~130 ms per user prompt (3 interpreters → 1), plus one shared rule-index/read of the prompt payload.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low-moderate: verify Claude Code accepts multiple additionalContext entries from one hook (single JSON with concatenated text is the safe form). One dispatcher bug silences all prompt-side nudges — keep per-check unit tests.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Feed a prompt that trips all three (numbered list + 不對 + a brain keyword) into the merged hook; assert the combined additionalContext contains all three payloads and hook_events.jsonl gets the same three event lines as the originals.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: UserPromptSubmit, every prompt (settings.json:44-46): regex-scan for CJK/English correction phrases; injects a reminder to invoke the self-improving skill.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Measured yield near zero: only 4 total events in hook_events.jsonl since 2026-05-15 (~7 weeks), of which just 1 was an actual inject. Compare siblings: check_numbered_list 546 injects, inject_method_brain 2,000.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~65 ms per prompt, every prompt — one of three python startups on the busiest human-facing event, paying full freight for ~1 firing per 7 weeks.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes — if kept at all, it must ride inside the merged prompt dispatcher, where its marginal cost is ~0 (one more regex pass).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: It is deterministic; the question is whether the trigger it feeds (self-improving skill invocation) actually happens — 1 inject in 7 weeks says the loop is not closing.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: Partially — CLAUDE.md/memory already instruct recording corrections; the hook adds little on top given its fire rate.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: Candidate: either the correction-phrase base rate collapsed (success!) or the SHORT_MSG_CHARS=200 + at-start-only soft-signal gates (check_correction_phrases.py:50-80) over-tightened it into uselessness. Decide with data, not vibes.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: As standalone removal: ~65 ms/prompt. Real gain is decision clarity: keep-in-dispatcher (free) vs delete (one less surface to maintain).
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low. Telemetry-and-nudge only; never blocks. Losing it loses a weak signal already near zero.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Run hook_stats.py --hook check_correction_phrases --days 60; separately grep recent session logs for the correction phrases themselves to distinguish 'no corrections happening' from 'hook thresholds too tight'.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: PreToolUse(Bash) every Bash call (settings.json:78-80): regex for codex/gemini stdout redirected uncapped into .ai/ (7 GB runaway incident 2026-04-17). On match it prints a warning to stderr and returns 0 (check_stdout_redirect.py:92-94).
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Effectively ZERO as wired: exit-0 hook stderr does not surface to Claude (the user's own check_redundant_reads.py:28-29 documents exactly this, which is why that hook was upgraded to exit-2). It is also the only hook with NO log_event call — no telemetry, so nobody can even measure whether it ever matches.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~65 ms python startup per Bash call for a warning nobody (human or model) ever sees.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes — into the Bash dispatcher (see quartet finding).
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: Either remove, or fix: upgrade to exit-2 block (matching the pattern's severity — 7 GB incident) and add log_event. Keeping it as-is is pure cost.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: If fixed inside the merged dispatcher: protection actually starts working at ~0 marginal cost. If removed: -65 ms/Bash call.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Upgrading to exit-2 could false-positive block; its DANGEROUS_PATTERNS are narrow (.ai/*.log targets) and SAFE_INDICATORS provide escapes, so risk is low. Doing nothing retains a latent-regression risk (the 7 GB pattern is unguarded in practice).
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Pipe the historical 7 GB-incident command shape into the hook and confirm (a) current build returns 0 with invisible stderr, (b) fixed build returns 2 with visible message; add a case to hooks/tests.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: PreToolUse(Bash): blocks raw `codex exec`/`gemini -p` and deprecated flags; 46 lifetime events (27 routing blocks, 19 deprecated-flag blocks) — a working, high-value gate.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: High: enforces the delegation contract that prose failed to enforce (181 raw vs 2 Skill calls = 99% bypass before blocking, check_codex_skill_routing.py:17-21).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~65 ms per Bash call (folds into dispatcher merge). The real cost found here is CORRECTNESS DRIFT: line 172 returns wrapper 'gemini-delegate' and build_block_message (lines 176-183) tells the agent to invoke Skill("gemini-delegate") — but CLAUDE.md declares gemini-delegate DEPRECATED 2026-06-18 and 'fails closed; do NOT route to it'. The hook now actively blocks a command and recommends a dead path.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — this hook exists because LLM reasoning bypassed the rule 99% of the time.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: No — but the .bak sibling should be (see hygiene finding).
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Prevents a guaranteed dead-end loop: block → agent obeys → Skill('gemini-delegate') fails closed → agent retries. Updating the gemini branch to 'route CJK judgment work to Claude-direct / bulk to codex-delegate' removes that loop.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low: message-text + wrapper-name change only; blocking regexes untouched.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Feed `gemini -p "do X"` payload; assert exit 2 and that the block message no longer names gemini-delegate as the fix; keep the codex-path golden test unchanged.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: UserPromptSubmit, every prompt: builds a trigger index by reading ALL 12 rule .md files (~65 KB) from the Obsidian vault, then matches the prompt. 2,000 injects logged — the highest-yield hook in the harness.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: High — this is the delivery mechanism that makes the method-brain vault causal (docstring, inject_method_brain.py:3-11).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~67 ms measured per fire. The _INDEX_CACHE at lines 81-82 ('cached and rebuilt only when the rules dir mtime changes') is dead weight: each hook fire is a fresh python process, so the module-level dict NEVER survives between prompts — every prompt re-reads every rule file. Harmless at 12 rules; grows linearly as the vault grows.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes — into the prompt dispatcher (which amortizes the interpreter, the dominant cost today).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — the point is injection before reasoning.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: Yes, properly: persist the built index to ~/.claude/logs/brain_index.json keyed by rules-dir mtime; a fresh process stats the dir, compares mtime, loads JSON instead of parsing 12+ markdown files. Also fix/remove the misleading in-process-cache comment.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Small today (~5-15 ms/prompt of file I/O + regex parse), but O(rules) — locks in flat cost as the vault grows past dozens of rules; and removes a comment that claims caching that does not exist.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low: mtime-keyed invalidation is already the designed semantic; a stale-cache bug degrades to injecting a slightly old rule text, fail-open.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Add rule file → next fire must reflect it (mtime bump invalidates); corrupt the cache JSON → hook must fall back to full rebuild, exit 0.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:105-114 — the ONLY '*'-matcher tool hook: fires on EVERY tool call (Read/Grep/Edit/Bash/MCP/...), tail-reads 200 lines of the 2.6 MB hook_events.jsonl looking for same-session brain injects in the last 10 min, and on match reads the rule file + keyword-scans the tool_input.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Moderate: 1,858 behavior_consistent events feeding brain_score.py's cue ladder at x1.3 (weak auto signal). Already noise-tightened in W22 (threshold 1→3 keywords, correlate_brain_evidence.py:79-85).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~70 ms measured per tool call — the single highest-frequency fork surface in the harness (thousands of fires/day). IMPORTANT: this wiring RESPECTS the d5f0bde rule as written (lightweight Python observer on '*' tolerated; Node is not) — no violation, but it is the per-fire budget to watch.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Not with the PreToolUse hooks (different event). Could absorb future PostToolUse observers.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: Already deterministic (docstring hard constraint: NO LLM, correlate_brain_evidence.py:22).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — it must be ambient and free of model involvement.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: Yes, cheaply: the common case (no recent inject this session) still pays the 200-line tail-read every fire. Have inject_method_brain touch a marker file (tmp/claude-brain-inject-<sid>) on inject; correlator stats that marker first and bails in <1 ms when absent or >10 min old.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: No, but re-evaluate at next /ai-review whether the weak x1.3 cue ever changed a promote/retire decision in brain_score.py — if it never has, this is the harness's most expensive telemetry-per-decision ratio.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Marker-file early-bail cuts the common-case work to a stat() — keeps per-tool-call overhead flat as hook_events.jsonl grows (tail-read is bounded but still opens/decodes a 2.6 MB file).
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low: marker is an optimization in front of unchanged logic; fail-open (marker missing → current tail-read path).
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Fire correlator with no marker → assert no jsonl open (strace/ProcMon or instrument); with fresh marker + matching haystack → assert identical behavior_consistent event as today.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: PreToolUse Read (every Read) and Bash (every Bash): session-scoped temp-file counters; block at 5th same-file read / 10th identical normalized command with exit-2 messages.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Highest measured ROI in the log: 1,171 read-blocks + 4,996 legitimate reset_on_modify exemptions + 25 bash-blocks. Each block prevents a redundant multi-KB file read or command re-run — token savings dwarf the 70 ms fork cost. Evidence base: 2,223 redundant reads/30d, 108x identical pytest (file headers).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: ~70 ms per Read / per Bash (the Bash side folds into the dispatcher merge). Temp state files are TTL-pruned (24 h) — no accumulation.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: check_redundant_bash → Bash dispatcher; check_redundant_reads stays solo on the Read matcher (only Read hook, nothing to merge with).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — the 30-day audit that motivated them is proof LLM self-monitoring failed here.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: State is already session-cached in temp files.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: None to extract beyond the Bash-dispatcher merge; these are the benchmark other hooks should be judged against (cheap deterministic gate, measurable yield, escape hatch, TTL'd state).
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Removing/loosening regresses to the documented 52x-same-file / 108x-same-command waste patterns.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Existing hooks/tests dir; plus periodic hook_stats.py check that block rate stays >0 (a silent wiring break would zero it).

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:120-123 runs it with --repo "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" --quiet on EVERY session start (all sources incl. compact). It os.walks the repo's .ai/ tree and can create per-month tar.gz archives + delete >200 MB runaway files (ai-workspace-organize.py:3-53). Daily logs in ~/.claude/logs/ (incl. a 04:00 Jul 6 entry) show a scheduled/nightly run also covers this.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Real hygiene (the 7 GB codex-stdout class of incident), but duplicated: the scheduled run already sweeps all REPOS nightly; the per-session run only adds 'catch runaway mid-day in the current repo'.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Python startup + full .ai/ tree walk + potential tarfile compression at interactive session start — the heaviest-write operation on any hook path. Cost scales with .ai/ size, exactly when the user is waiting for a session to open. Also fires on 'compact' (mid-session).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: Yes: keep ONE owner. Either scheduled-task-only (recommended), or per-session but stripped to the cheap runaway-file check (size scan only, no archiving).
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: A per-day debounce (like check_ai_review_due's DEBOUNCE_STATE, check_ai_review_due.py:80) would make repeat session-starts free.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: The SessionStart wiring is the removal candidate; the script itself stays.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Removes tree-walk + possible tar compression from interactive session-open latency; halves the execution surface (one owner, one log stream).
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: A runaway file created mid-day waits until the nightly sweep (worst case ~24 h × growth rate). If that is unacceptable, keep a size-only guard at SessionStart (<5 ms).
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Time SessionStart with a populated .ai/ (e.g. 500 MB) before/after; verify nightly scheduled run still archives and the daily log file still appears.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:130-165 wires the same bash heredoc-cat on FOUR matchers, injecting 'CRITICAL - ALWAYS use codebase-memory-mcp tools FIRST for ANY code exploration' into context at every session boundary and every compaction.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: Negative-to-zero net: CLAUDE.md's own codebase-memory rule explicitly says results are ADVISORY (4/5 no-silent-pass probes failed) and calls THIS hook out by name as the 'blunter... no caveat' form it corrects. The user thus pays context tokens every session for an instruction their governing doc contradicts.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Execution cost trivial (bash cat, ~10-30 ms). Real cost is context: ~120 tokens injected per session start AND per compact (compaction is exactly when context budget is scarcest), plus instruction-conflict noise.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: The four identical matcher entries could at minimum collapse to one '*' entry; better, the content should defer to the CLAUDE.md scoped rule.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: N/A — it's a static text inject.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: Yes — CLAUDE.md already carries the correct, scoped version of this instruction; the hook adds only the incorrect unscoped version.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: Yes, or rewrite its text to match the CLAUDE.md advisory form (verify-load-bearing-claims, re-index-before-query, skip-for-small-repos). Removal is simplest since CLAUDE.md is always in context.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: ~120 context tokens per session start + per compaction reclaimed; eliminates a standing instruction conflict the model must resolve every session.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Low: codebase-memory-mcp usage may dip; CLAUDE.md's scoped rule (which the user prefers) still triggers it for large/unfamiliar repos. The installer may re-add the hook on cbm upgrade — recheck after upgrades.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Remove for 2 weeks; compare codebase-memory-mcp tool-call frequency in session logs before/after (mcp__codebase-memory-mcp__* grep) to confirm the CLAUDE.md rule alone sustains appropriate usage.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: hooks/cbm-code-discovery-gate exists (executable, calls codebase-memory-mcp.exe hook-augment per fire) but is wired NOWHERE in global settings — grep of settings.json and settings.local.json found zero references. UNVERIFIED: could be wired in some per-repo .claude/settings.json not scanned by this lane.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: None while unwired; if wired it would add an exe spawn to a PreToolUse path (search augmentation).
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Zero runtime today; nonzero confusion cost (an auditor/tool inventorying hooks/ counts a gate that never fires; the filename says 'gate' but the body says it never blocks).
inefficient_invocations.should_be_removed: Yes, unless a per-repo settings.json is found referencing it — decide deliberately: wire it (and measure the exe spawn cost per fire) or delete the file.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Inventory clarity only; prevents a future accidental wiring of an unmeasured exe-per-tool-call cost.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Near zero if truly unwired; run the suggested test first.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: grep -r "cbm-code-discovery-gate" across all repos' .claude/settings*.json before deleting; if any hit, measure `codebase-memory-mcp.exe hook-augment` latency before keeping it wired.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: settings.json:168-172: dedicated statusLine runs `bash statusline.sh` with refreshInterval 1. Script is explicitly fork-minimized (statusline.sh:6-11: printf -v globals, one jq call, one git call) and has VL_CLOCK_SECONDS=1 (line 33), i.e. it is DESIGNED to re-render every second for a seconds-precision clock.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: High UX value (context %, rate limits, cost, git state) — this is the legitimate replacement for statusline_pro.mjs.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: Per render on Windows Git Bash: ~3 forks (bash + jq + git). If refreshInterval 1 means 1 second (UNVERIFIED — Claude Code docs for this field's unit were not checkable from this offline lane), that is ~10,800 forks/hour of idle session — continuous background fork load on the same Windows fork-cost budget the d5f0bde incident was about.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: The git segment could cache by HEAD/index mtime, but the cheaper lever is refresh rate.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Setting VL_CLOCK_SECONDS=0 (or VL_CLOCK=off) and refreshInterval 5-10 cuts idle fork load ~80-90% with the only visible loss being a seconds-ticking clock.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Cosmetic only (clock granularity, slightly staler ctx%/cost numbers between renders).
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Verify refreshInterval units in Claude Code docs/changelog first; then watch process-creation rate (e.g. Windows Performance Monitor 'Process/Elapsed' or a 60 s ProcMon capture) at interval 1 vs 5.

inefficient_invocations.invocation_name: code-reviewer subagent (tool scope + deterministic parity check embedded in LLM pass)
inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: agents/code-reviewer.md — mandatory per CLAUDE.md on nearly every shipping commit (≥2 files or ≥50 LOC). Runs on sonnet with tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep. Its first mandated step is the cross-implementation parity check: a mechanical `git grep -nF "$OLD"` for surviving copies of a bumped value (code-reviewer.md:26-35).
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: High (the v0.88.15 4-P1-bugs lesson justifies the LLM review). But two efficiency defects: (1) a REVIEWER is granted Write+Edit (code-reviewer.md:4) — wider than its read-and-report contract, and wider scope = more tool schemas in its context per spawn; (2) the parity check is 100% deterministic yet re-derived by an LLM on every review invocation.
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: One sonnet subagent per shipping commit (correctly cheap vs opus). The parity-check portion burns LLM turns (choose OLD/NEW, run grep, interpret) that a script does in <1 s with zero tokens.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_merged: The parity check should merge into the existing pre-commit review-gate hook (AGENTS.md commit-gates workflow) as a deterministic step; the subagent then only adjudicates survivors the script found.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: The parity half: yes — a parity_check.py that diffs staged changes for changed constants/versions and git-greps for old-value survivors, emitting JSON for the reviewer to adjudicate. The judgment half (is this survivor legitimate?): no.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: The judgment half already is LLM; the mechanical half should not be.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Drop Write/Edit from tools (smaller spawn context, removes an unneeded mutation capability from a 'read-only' role); scripting the parity check saves several LLM tool-turns per review and makes the check non-skippable (hook-enforced instead of prompt-enforced).
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Removing Write/Edit: none for review output (it reports, caller fixes). Scripted parity check may over-report survivors; keep the LLM adjudication step so false positives cost one sentence, not a block.
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: Replay the 33340ed/70e6fdc gpt-5.4→5.5 incident diff through the parity script and assert it flags run_codex.sh:49 + run_codex.ps1:4; spawn the narrowed-tools reviewer on a recent commit and confirm identical verdict quality.

inefficient_invocations.current_usage_scenario: hook_events.jsonl (2.6 MB, 10.7k lines) is aggregated by deterministic scripts: hook_stats.py (per-hook/per-day/per-session counts, --json) and ai_review_scan.py (memory-recurrence, CLAUDE.md health, skill deadweight, --json, read-only). The /ai-review skill (LLM) consumes these for its periodic report.
inefficient_invocations.value_provided: This is the CORRECT division of labor already in place: deterministic JSON emitters for counting, LLM only for judgment/prioritization. No evidence of the anti-pattern the audit probed for (LLM regenerating reports from raw logs) — the scripts exist precisely to prevent it (ai_review_scan.py:3-5: 'the not-yet-scripted scans for the /ai-review command').
inefficient_invocations.likely_cost: On-demand only (no per-fire cost). hook_events.jsonl grows unbounded (~2.6 MB / ~8 weeks ≈ 17 MB/year) — the only latent cost, since correlate_brain_evidence opens it every tool call (bounded tail-read, but open+seek cost grows).
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_deterministic_code: Already is, for the countable parts.
inefficient_invocations.can_be_replaced_by_LLM_reasoning: No — moving counting back into the LLM would be a regression.
inefficient_invocations.should_be_cached: The log should be ROTATED (monthly, e.g. hook_events-2026-06.jsonl) so tail-reads and aggregations stay flat; scripts already accept --since/--days windows.
inefficient_invocations.expected_efficiency_gain: Only the rotation: keeps the every-tool-call jsonl open cost flat forever; otherwise this pipeline is the pattern the rest of the harness should copy.
inefficient_invocations.risk_if_changed: Rotation must preserve the correlator's 10-minute lookback (rotate at day boundaries, or have correlator check current file only — it already does).
inefficient_invocations.suggested_test: After adding rotation, run hook_stats.py --days 30 spanning a rotation boundary and assert counts match the pre-rotation baseline.
